cluster_name: Armed gunman shot dead at White House security checkpoint
tldr: Headlines across all 13 country-buckets converge almost entirely on the security-event register — gunman, checkpoint, Secret Service kills — producing far less interpretive scatter than most stories of comparable salience. The sharpest headline-layer divergences are: RT labels the suspect 'allegedly delusional' rather than a security threat; Hindustan Times frames the entire incident as part of 'Donald Trump's wild weekend' (wedding, Iran deal, then gunfire); and News24 and Republic World both lead with 'Trump safe', elevating presidential protection to the dominant news value rather than the security breach itself.
event_summary: On May 23, 2026, Nasire Best, 21, approached the White House checkpoint at 17th Street and Pennsylvania Avenue NW, drew a revolver, and opened fire on Secret Service officers; agents returned fire and killed him. A bystander was struck and hospitalized while President Trump remained unharmed inside.

frames.sub_frame: White House checkpoint breach, gunman killed

evidence.quote: Secret Service shoots person near White House
Suspect killed after opening fire on Secret Service
US Secret Service kills gunman in shootout near White House

frames.sub_frame: Shooter's prior history and violent record

evidence.quote: Shooter's multiple past encounters with Secret Service
Gunman had 'violent history and possible obsession'
Son's wedding skipped, Iran deal teased, then gunfire
Trump safe as US Secret Service kills gunman
White House Checkpoint With Weapon, Trump Safe
Bystander wounded in shooting near White House
Thoughts with bystander shot after gunfire exchanged
Allegedly delusional gunman fatally shot near White House

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to characterise the suspect as 'allegedly delusional' in the headline, using a mental-health dismissal rather than any security-threat or White House-perimeter framing — conspicuously avoiding any systemic-vulnerability narrative.

Only headline in the corpus to embed the shooting within Trump's full political weekend — wedding skipped, Iran deal teased, then gunfire — making it the sole title that contextualises the incident as one beat in a larger presidential narrative rather than a standalone security event.

Leads with journalists ducking for cover ('Journalists Duck For Cover As Gunfire Erupts') — the only headline to foreground eyewitness chaos over the security outcome, foregrounding spectacle rather than the gunman killed or bystander injured.

Most stripped headline in the corpus: 'Gunshots Heard Outside White House' — passive witness framing with no mention of the gunman killed, the bystander shot, or the Secret Service response; minimises the breach to an ambient sound event.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, the White House shooting story is almost uniformly rendered as a security event — gunman killed at checkpoint, Secret Service responds — with virtually no cross-outlet interpretive divergence; the notable exceptions are RT's dismissive 'allegedly delusional' label, Hindustan Times' political-narrative framing across Trump's entire weekend, and the 'Trump safe' presidential-protection hook favoured by South African and Indian right-populist outlets.
